title: Humor in AI: Massive Scale Crowd-Sourced Preferences and Benchmarks for Cartoon Captioning
research_questions: Can current LLMs recognize humor and generate amusing captions that resonate with human audiences?
Can the vision-language integration in MLLMs be further improved to close the gap with language-only counterparts in caption generation?
Can we train a reward model able to better capture humor, and can RLHF still be effectively applied to settings where the distribution of correct responses is highly sensitive?
Does DPO lead to better in-distribution generation, and produce a model more effectively able to match the distribution of the finetuning data?
Does the data diversity used for finetuning explain DPO's surprisingly good diversity metrics as compared to PPO and SFT, or are other mechanisms at play?
tasks: preference-based fine-tuning for humorous caption generation

datasets_used.name: New Yorker caption ranking (Humor in AI)
New Yorker Caption Contest corpus
datasets_used.size: contests up to #763; testing (47 contests) and validation split (44 contests) used in the 91-contest test split

methods_proposed.name: New Yorker caption ranking dataset
methods_proposed.summary: A large-scale multimodal preference dataset from The New Yorker weekly cartoon caption contest, containing cartoons, submitted captions, and crowd ratings collected through an online rating system over contests 530 to 895.

methods_proposed.name: HumorousAI Benchmark
methods_proposed.summary: A benchmark for funny cartoon caption generation in which models generate ten captions per held-out cartoon and are compared against human submission groups ranked #1-10, #200-209, #1000-1009, and median.

methods_proposed.name: Group Comparison (Overall)
methods_proposed.summary: An evaluation method where a human or LLM judge compares two groups of ten captions and chooses the group that is funnier overall; the paper selects GPT4-Turbo with Hessel et al. descriptions for this benchmark setting.

methods_proposed.name: Group Comparison (Best Pick)
methods_proposed.summary: An evaluation method where the judge first identifies the funniest caption in each group and then chooses which group's best caption is funnier; the paper selects GPT4o-vision with raw cartoon images for this benchmark setting.

methods_proposed.name: Preference-pair construction for caption alignment
methods_proposed.summary: Preference data are built by pairing captions for the same cartoon where the preferred caption has a higher human rating and is at least three combined standard deviations above the rejected caption.

methods_proposed.name: GPT evaluator recalibration
methods_proposed.summary: For group comparisons without chain of thought, the authors recalibrate GPT4 A/B decisions by choosing a validation-set threshold on log-probability differences to reduce option-position bias.

evaluation_methods.detail: The New Yorker crowdsourced ratings use a UCB-variant bandit algorithm; users rate captions as funny (reward 3), somewhat funny (reward 2), or unfunny (reward 1), producing rankings and preference data.

GPT4-family evaluators are tested in pairwise, Group Overall, and Group Best Pick settings, using 5-shot in-context prompting; the main benchmark uses GPT4-Turbo with Hessel et al. descriptions for Overall and GPT4o-vision with raw images for Best Pick.

Prolific crowd workers evaluate caption comparisons, including pairwise/group reliability experiments and Claude-3-Opus versus Human Top 10 comparisons; participants gave informed consent and were paid $12 an hour.

A former New Yorker cartoon editor evaluates group comparisons and Claude-3-Opus versus Human Top 10 captions; the expert achieves 94.28±2.79 ranking accuracy in Group Overall with cartoon images and prefers Claude only 1.6% of the time.

Caption diversity is measured with expectation-adjusted distinct N-grams (Average EAD) and Sentence-BERT embedding cosine similarity (SBERT); reward models are also compared using Reward Ranking Acc (%).

headline_results.finding: The released dataset contains 365 contests/cartoons, over 2.2M captions, and 284,183,913 human ratings.
headline_results.dataset: New Yorker caption ranking (Humor in AI)

headline_results.finding: The selected GPT4-Turbo Overall group evaluator with Hessel et al. descriptions identified captions ranked #1-10 over #1000-1009 with 77.5±2.96 ranking accuracy, compared with 67±3.33 for the best Pairwise GPT4-Turbo setting and 94.28±2.79 for the human expert.
headline_results.dataset: New Yorker caption ranking (Humor in AI)

headline_results.finding: Human workers preferred Claude-3-Opus generated caption groups over Human Top 10 captions 35.4% of the time, while the human expert preferred Claude only 1.6% of the time.
headline_results.dataset: New Yorker caption ranking (Humor in AI)

headline_results.finding: Under GPT-based Overall group evaluation, Claude-3-Opus was the strongest closed-source generator, with win rates of 54.40, 70.88, 81.87, and 88.46 against Top 10, #200-#209, #1000-#1009, and Contestant Median groups.
headline_results.dataset: New Yorker caption ranking (Humor in AI)

headline_results.finding: Among Mistral variants, DPO was best on Best Pick Win Rate, while BoN was best on three Overall Win Rate comparisons.
headline_results.metric: Best Pick Win Rate (%) / Overall Win Rate (%)
headline_results.dataset: New Yorker caption ranking (Humor in AI)
headline_results.best_system: Mistral-7B Instruct v0.1 DPO; Mistral-7B Instruct v0.1 BoN

headline_results.finding: For diversity, Mistral DPO substantially exceeded Mistral BoN and approached Human Top 10 on the reported metrics.
headline_results.value: Human (Top 10): 0.9456 / 0.7452; Mistral-7B Instruct DPO: 0.9206 / 0.7075; Mistral-7B Instruct BoN: 0.8663 / 0.4868
headline_results.dataset: New Yorker caption ranking (Humor in AI)
headline_results.best_system: Human (Top 10); Mistral-7B Instruct DPO among Mistral variants

key_findings: The paper releases a large-scale human preference dataset for cartoon captions, with 365 contests, over 2.2M captions, and 284,183,913 ratings.
Group-based evaluation makes GPT-based humor judging more reliable than pairwise caption comparison, but a human expert still substantially outperforms both GPT models and average crowd workers.

Closed-source models can generate captions that GPT evaluators often prefer over lower-ranked human submissions, but human workers and especially the expert still strongly prefer Human Top 10 captions over Claude-3-Opus.

Language-only models outperform multimodal counterparts in several settings, and the authors find that adding visual inputs to GPT4-Turbo-vision negatively biases ranking accuracy relative to text-only descriptions.

For open-source Mistral models, SFT generally hurts humor-generation performance, BoN improves Overall Win Rate, DPO improves Best Pick Win Rate, and PPO/RLHF increases reward-model scores without corresponding humor gains.
Human captions are more diverse than model-generated captions, and Mistral DPO yields the strongest diversity metrics among the Mistral finetuning variants.
limitations: Humor judgments are subjective, and average human crowd workers are not very reliable predictors of top-ranked captions.
The main benchmark depends on GPT-based evaluators, which remain below the human expert and require recalibration for A/B option bias.
The study focuses on The New Yorker cartoon caption contest, so the dataset and benchmark reflect that domain's audience, editorial filtering, and caption style.
The authors state that supervised fine-tuning aligns to captions in the top 1000, most of which are not particularly funny, which can hurt generation quality.
The authors hypothesize that RLHF/PPO struggles because humor is sensitive to small wording changes and reward models may give erroneous signals on out-of-distribution generations.
The paper identifies humor versus offensiveness as an important future issue rather than solving that tradeoff.
safety_ethics_notes: The New Yorker editorial staff filters captions that are not humorous or include personal information and/or offensive content before crowdsourced rating. Human experiments used informed consent and paid participants $12 an hour. The authors note that optimizing humor may increase offensiveness and toxicity, and they identify balancing humor with offensiveness and cultural sensitivity as future work.

artifacts.other: Dataset released under Creative Commons Attribution Non Commercial 4.0; codebase released under Apache 2.0.
